skill.name: creating-hooks
skill.description: Guides users through creating Claude Code hooks for workflow automation, event handling, hook configuration, and reusable shell script templates.
skill.summary: Create Claude Code hooks with event guidance, settings examples, and shell templates.
skill.author: BrendanShields

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, and sensitive-file patterns. Review found these are mostly documented Claude Code hook examples and shell templates, not hidden malware, but the skill still enables automatic shell execution and project file inspection. Publish with a warning that hook scripts run with local project access and must be reviewed before installation.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Shell Hook Execution Requires Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill teaches users to register bash commands as Claude Code hooks and includes executable shell templates. This is legitimate for a hook-authoring skill, but installed hooks can run automatically during tool use or session events with local project access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook command examples and shell templates are explicit and executable. The context shows intended automation, not hidden execution, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Hooks Can Inspect Project Files and Metadata
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The templates inspect project files, hidden project state, git metadata, and protected filename patterns. This is useful for automation, but it gives hooks visibility into local repository structure and sensitive file names.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file checks and git command are directly present in templates. They do not transmit data, but they do access local project context.

low_findings.title: Static High-Severity Indicators Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The scanner flagged weak cryptography and command-substitution patterns in markdown examples and shell templates. Review found no cryptographic implementation, no C2 behavior, and no prompt-injection text; the apparent indicators are hook documentation, shell examples, and protective filename patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show security guidance and defensive file blocking. No evidence found for weak cryptographic use, command-and-control behavior, or malicious obfuscation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands Configured as Hooks
dangerous_patterns.description: Hook configuration runs bash scripts from Claude Code settings. This is expected for the skill, but users should treat copied hook scripts as executable code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command strings are explicit and are central to hook installation. The pattern is risky because it creates automatic execution paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Context Injection Into Claude Code Sessions
dangerous_patterns.description: The SessionStart template can add project-derived context to Claude Code. This is useful, but incorrect customization could expose sensitive project details in the session context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The template intentionally builds additionalContext from project state. There is no exfiltration, but the pattern can disclose local metadata inside the model context.

content.value_statement: Claude Code hooks can automate repeated checks, context setup, and file protection, but setup details are easy to miss. This skill gives event guidance, configuration examples, and reusable shell templates for safer hook creation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code hook events and when each event runs.
Shows settings.json structures for registering hook commands.
Provides shell templates for basic hooks, context injection, and file protection.
Documents exit codes and JSON output decisions for hook control.
Includes examples for testing hooks manually with sample input.
limitations: Requires users to review and customize shell scripts before use.
Assumes jq, bash, and Claude Code hook support are available.
Does not provide a full hook testing framework or installer.
Does not guarantee hook compatibility with future Claude Code versions.

use_cases.title: Protect Sensitive Project Files
use_cases.description: Add a PreToolUse hook that blocks edits to environment files, key files, and other protected paths.

use_cases.title: Inject Project Context at Startup
use_cases.description: Create a SessionStart hook that adds language, feature, or branch context when a Claude Code session begins.

use_cases.title: Prototype Hook-Based Workflow Automation
use_cases.description: Use the basic template and event reference to design custom hooks for validation, logging, or review workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Hook Event
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the right Claude Code hook event for a workflow that should run before file edits.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple Claude Code hook configuration and shell script that logs successful Write tool usage.

prompt_templates.title: Protect Sensitive Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a PreToolUse hook that blocks edits to environment files, credential files, and private key files.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Event Hook Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Claude Code hook workflow that injects startup context, validates tool outputs, and records session cleanup actions.

output_examples.input: User asks for a hook that blocks edits to secrets.
output_examples.output: A PreToolUse plan with protected filename patterns, blocking behavior, setup steps, and a manual test command.

output_examples.input: User asks which hook event should add context when work begins.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to use SessionStart, with the expected input fields and the shape of the returned context.

output_examples.input: User asks how to debug a hook.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering sample stdin input, exit code checks, verbose mode, and stderr behavior.

best_practices: Review every hook script as executable local code before enabling it.
Keep hook matchers narrow so scripts run only for the intended events or tools.
Validate file paths, quote variables, and set clear timeouts for hook commands.
anti_patterns: Do not use broad matchers for hooks that modify or block tool behavior.
Do not log prompts, secrets, or file contents unless users explicitly approve it.
Do not copy templates into production without customizing protected paths and tests.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code hooks for automation, validation, context injection, and workflow customization.

faq.question: Does it install hooks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates that users must copy, review, configure, and test.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: Hooks can run shell scripts automatically with project access. The reviewed files are not malicious, but scripts need careful review.

faq.question: Which hook events are covered?
faq.answer: It covers events such as PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, SessionStart, SessionEnd, Stop, and related events.

faq.question: Can the templates protect secret files?
faq.answer: Yes. The file-protector template shows how to block edits to environment files, credentials, private keys, and selected paths.

faq.question: What tools are needed to use the templates?
faq.answer: The templates assume bash and jq are available, and some examples use git for project metadata.
